# UC-19.1.1 · Blade/Rack Server Health (Cisco UCS)

> **Criticality:** Critical &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Availability, Reliability &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We read the hardware health list Cisco rack and blade servers send to the unified platform manager, using the part location in the long system name, not a word guess from a short label. We sort by that path so a fan, power unit, or memory bank is fixed while redundancy still holds, and we keep the fault number for faster runbook routing.*

---

## Description

Flags **active** Cisco UCS Manager `faultInst` events whose `dn` sits under in-scope B-series chasses (`sys/chassis-*/blade-*`) or rack servers (`sys/rack-unit-*`), with hardware component buckets derived from `dn` substrings, lifecycle filtered to non-cleared `lc` states, and optional analyst drill-down on `code` and the `cause` enum for runbook mapping.

## Value

Spotting failing field-replaceable units and sub-critical thermal or power issues while redundancy still masks guest workloads avoids surprise hypervisor or bare-metal node loss, preserves time to ship parts, and gives application owners a clean change window before an uncorrectable error or a full power loss exhausts the remaining healthy path in the same server.

## Implementation

Enable `cisco:ucs:faults` from `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` (API and/or correlated syslog), scope DNs to blades and registered rack units, and schedule a deduplicated alert on `major`/`critical` (optionally `warning` for SRE) with throttling on `dn`+`code`.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, Splunkbase 2731, v4.3.1 or newer) on the Heavy Forwarder or other node that is permitted to run modular inputs, with outbound HTTPS to each UCS Manager cluster virtual IP, plus the same add-on (or a TA-only bundle) on Search Heads for saved-search knowledge objects, lookups, and field extractions.
- A dedicated UCS Manager **service account** for read-only API access. Before production, in UCSM Admin assign a role that can read the **fault and equipment** subtrees, including the `faultInst` class used for live open faults. Do not use named-user administrator credentials. Store the password in Splunk’s credential store when the add-on supports that integration; otherwise protect `local/inputs.conf` with host filesystem ACLs. For syslog-only architectures you still need a read-only user for **validation in Step 3** even if collection does not authenticate for every event line.
- Network path: 443/tcp from the collection host to `https://<ucsm-fqdn>/` without TLS forward-proxy breakage; if a corporate proxy is mandatory, create an allow-list for the UCSM nodes. For syslog, open 514/udp|tcp or the agreed TLS syslog profile between UCSM, the data-center switches, and the receiving forwarder.
- An index the organisation approves for Cisco UCS, typically `cisco_ucs`, and a Splunk role whose `srchIndexesAllowed` includes that index. Budget license from fault volume: usually modest versus wire-speed NetFlow, but high-severity events during upgrades can burst.
- Inventory knowledge: which servers are Intersight-led versus classic UCSM-led so the `exclusions` paragraph is not a surprise in audits.

### Step 1 — Configure data collection (modular input and/or UCSM syslog)
(1) **Modular input (UCSM XML API polling for `faultInst`)** — after install, open `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/default/inputs.conf` and copy the **documented** stanza template for the Cisco UCS family (naming is commonly `cisco_ucs://<label>`; exact keys for URL, user, and index are versioned—do not improvise a stanza that does not exist in the file you are shipped). Re-create that template under `local/inputs.conf`, set `disabled = 0`, set the URL to `https://<ucsm-fqdn>` (or the pair of cluster peers if the add-on documents paired endpoints), and bind the destination index, usually `cisco_ucs`, while preserving the **sourcetype** the TA applies to fault MOs, normally `cisco:ucs:faults`. `splunk restart` the forwarder or use the **Manage** action for the modular input in Splunk Web. Tail `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/splunkd.log` for python errors naming the add-on; fix TLS first if you see `CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED` or 401/403.
(2) **UCSM syslog (recommended parallel or alternate path)** — in UCSM choose **Admin > Communication & Management > Communication Services** (wording can vary by 3.x/4.x) and configure a **syslog** destination to your site collector. Set severities to include **fault** and **equipment** classes per Cisco guidance, name the **facility** your SecOps will not strip, and test with a one-line logger from another host before relying on production chassis events. On the receiving Universal Forwarder, set **props/transforms** so the stream is parsed as `cisco:ucs:faults` **or** a clearly named child sourcetype, and if you use a child sourcetype, union it in the base SPL. Treat syslog as **near-real-time**; API as **authoritative** for field completeness. If you wire **both** paths, de-duplicate in post-processing using `code`, `dn`, and `_time` windowing so on-call is not paged twice for the same FRU.
(3) **Prove fields before you build finals** — run a probe: `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-4h` then `| head 20` and use the **Fields** sidebar, plus `| stats count by sourcetype` and `| stats count by classId` only if that field appears on fault rows in your TA build. If `lc` is missing, keep the time filter tight and re-check TA release notes: some levels emit only create events.

### Step 2 — Create the search, report, and alert
Paste the `spl` field from this use case. **Design note — why `dn` and not `cause` substrings for component:** Cisco’s `faultInst.cause` is an enumerated value such as `equipment-degraded` or `link-down`, not a prose description, so a pattern `like(cause,"%cpu%")` never matches real events. The **Distinguished Name** string carries the managed-object path, including concrete anchors such as `/board/cpu-2` or `/memarray-1/mem-5` or `/psu-1`. The search lowercases `dn` once, then classifies in decreasing specificity (CPU and memory and disk before a broad “Other” bucket). Use **`code` + Cisco’s fault catalogue** as a *secondary* router to vendor maintenance articles (examples you can bookmark while testing: **F0479** for memory, **F0521** for CPU thermal, **F0408** for PSU input). The **`cause` enum** is still useful to group by behaviour (`power-problem` vs `link-down`) even when two codes land in the same component bucket. For paging, add `| where severity IN ("major","critical")` (and tune) **after** you confirm how your TA normalises `severity` case. Throttle on `dn` and `code` to absorb soak timers.

### Step 3 — Validate against Cisco UCS Manager
(1) Copy one Splunk row’s `code` and full `dn`. Example memory scenario: a `dn` ending in `.../memarray-1/mem-4/fault-F0479` with **F0479** present.
(2) In UCSM GUI, open the **Equipment** view, navigate the tree to the same chassis, blade, or rack object implied by the left-hand `sys/…` path, then open the object’s **Faults** list (4.x: select the object in **Equipment**; use the **Faults** tab, or the global **Equipment → Faults** list with filters, depending on your version). The **code** and description seed must match. If the GUI is clean but Splunk still shows open lifecycle, you are on the wrong domain FQDN, looking at a stale read-only user cache, or seeing replication lag between cluster peers.
(3) Optional XML cross-check: from an automation host with the same read-only user, a read-only `configResolveClass` query for `faultInst` for that `dn` should return the same tuple as the add-on, modulo poll skew.

### Step 4 — Operationalise
(1) Build a dashboard that follows the `visualization` list: a **table or status grid** keyed on a short server anchor you `rex` from `dn` (`sys/chassis-…/blade-…` or `sys/rack-unit-…`), a **bar chart** of `component` counts, a **single value** of open **critical** rows grouped by `code`, a second **table** for `ack` versus active lifecycle, and a **timechart** of `severity` counts. Default the timepicker to 24h for NOC, 7d for problem management.
(2) Alerting: pass `dn`, `code`, `component`, `descr`, and a deep link to the Cisco **Faults Reference** row for the code. If your CMDB has chassis owners, `lookup` from the extracted `chassis-#` to route tickets.
(3) For maintenance: join against a `maintenance_windows` lookup for chassis and rack DNs. Do not mark acknowledged Splunk events as “safe” with `ack=yes` in UCSM without local policy—UCSM can acknowledge a fault that is still not cleared.

### Step 5 — Troubleshooting
- **Zero `cisco:ucs:faults` events in the index** — modular input not enabled, app only on a Search Head without a licensed execution context, 401/403, wrong index name in the search, or blocked network. Start at `_internal` logs filtered on the add-on’s python class name, then `curl -vk https://<ucsm-fqdn>/` from the same host to isolate TLS.
- **Events exist, but `dn` is null** — a broken props/transform on syslog can shred structure; compare one `_raw` line against a TA poller event. Fix sourcetype routing first.
- **All components are `Other`** — MO paths in your environment differ (for example, extra `/board` nesting). Add two carefully reviewed `like(ldn, …)` clauses after capturing five real DNs. Never rely on the deprecated `like(cause,"%cpu%")` shortcut.
- **Duplicate pages for a single event** — syslog plus API. Pick one **primary** feed for paging or hard-dedup with `| dedup` using `dn`+`code` in a 60-second window, understanding this can hide a genuine re-arm—tune the window to how quickly UCS oscillates in your site.
- **`lc=cleared` still showing** — the search filter should have removed these; re-run with `| search lc!=cleared`. If the TA re-ingests full snapshots, you may be seeing a historical run—narrow `earliest` to last poll interval for alert windows.
- **“Ghost” open faults in Splunk after clear in UI** — poll interval is longer than the operator’s re-check, or the syslog clear message took an alternate path. Reconcile indexes and time skew using `_indextime` vs `_time` plots for that `dn`.
- **Severity appears as unexpected strings** — some integrations emit mixed case; add `| eval sev=lower(severity)` in a temporary panel before changing production alerts.
- **Read-only user sees only fabric faults, not server FRUs** — RBAC is scoped; extend the read role to the equipment org that owns blades and racks, or the fault is invisible by design.
- **Upgrade waves flood minor faults** — temporary voltage or sensor warnings while firmware synchronises. Combine an organisational change calendar lookup with a higher `count` of repeats before paging (`occur>1` if your TA forwards `occur` reliably).
- **Validation mismatch only on C-series** — C-series that are **not** registered to the UCSM you poll will never show here; they are a data-source gap, not a search bug.


## SPL

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-24h@h
| search (dn="sys/chassis-*/blade-*" OR dn="sys/rack-unit-*")
| where (lc!="cleared" OR isnull(lc))
| eval ldn=lower(dn)
| eval component=case(
    match(ldn, "/board/cpu-\d"), "CPU",
    like(ldn, "%/memarray-%"), "Memory",
    like(ldn, "%/disk-%"), "Disk",
    like(ldn, "%/adaptor-%"), "Adapter",
    like(ldn, "%/board/storage-%"), "StorageController",
    like(ldn, "%/psu-%"), "PSU",
    like(ldn, "%/fan-%"), "Fan",
    like(ldn, "%/mgmt%"), "Management",
    1=1, "Other"
  )
| stats count, max(created) as last_fault_time, first(severity) as severity, first(lc) as lc, first(type) as type, first(code) as code, first(cause) as cause, first(descr) as descr, first(ack) as ack by dn, component
| sort 0 - count, - severity, dn
```

## Visualization

(1) Status grid: colour tiles by max(`severity`) per `rex`-extracted `server_mo` anchor from `dn` (`sys/chassis-…/blade-…` or `sys/rack-unit-…`). (2) Horizontal bar: fault row counts by `component` (CPU, Memory, Disk, …). (3) Single value: number of `severity=critical` rows in the time window, second tile by distinct `code`. (4) Table: `ack`, `lc`, `code`, `cause`, `component`, `dn`, `descr` for operator sort. (5) Timechart: event counts split by `severity` for storm detection during firmware waves.

## Known False Positives

Transient **I2C/SEL glitches** right after a blade re-seats that clear within a few poll cycles, looking severe in Splunk for minutes while the GUI already shows **Soaking** state. **Dual-feed architectures** (API plus syslog) can emit the same `code`+`dn` twice with offset timestamps, so deduplication and ownership of a single *authoritative* feed are essential before judging noise. **Firmware activation** and **CIMC reset** open bursts of `equipment-degraded` or `equipment-inoperable` that self-resolve per Cisco soak timers, yet the ticket still looks “red” in email if the alert has no `lc` or `occur` awareness. **Second fabric interconnect** maintenance often raises `link-down` or `equipment-missing` on paths that are still redundant—operations teams in healthy clusters will acknowledge these while the maintenance banner is on. **Chassis re-acknowledge and discovery** after a truck roll can list historical-looking faults the UI filters differently than the raw `faultInst` list your TA last polled, especially if poll frequency is 15 minutes but humans refresh in 15 seconds. **Time-zone and clock skew** between UCSM, the forwarder, and Splunk indexers can make a *cleared* event appear *after* the *raised* event in a naive table sort; always compare in UCSM local time for Sev1, not only `_time` order. **Read-only scoping** may hide a rack unit a database admin is staring at in the all-admin GUI—alerts on missing objects are a permissions story, not a false positive, but it feels like one until fixed. **Guest OS** disk or memory errors are out of scope here and must never be conflated with `faultInst` on the service profile equipment tree.
